onboarding.quote: Onboarding is the only part of your product experience that a hundred percent of people are ever going to touch.
Your brand is the promise you're making and your product experience is your delivery of that promise. Those two things have to be in lockstep.
If you're not prototyping and building to see what you want to build, I think you're doing it wrong.

growth.quote: Growth as the job is to connect users to the value of your product. Growth sometimes gets this reputation that it's just pure metrics hacking.
Start by assuming you no longer have product market fit, because you had product market fit in a different market.
We rarely scale a project until we know the Sean Ellis score hit a threshold that we find really compelling.

retention.quote: User retention is gold for consumer subscription companies. If you don't retain your users, then a lot of the onus is on getting them to pay on day one.
In product-led sales, product acquires and activates a customer and product creates pipeline for sales.
Good luck getting a hundred percent feature adoption of anything else in your product.

pricing.quote: The good founders need to be able to dominate both market share and wallet share. It is not a choice. You need to get better at both.
Do not set it and forget it. Think about your pricing just like you do your roadmap. Every 6 to 12 months, there's probably something meaningful you're leaving on the table.
The most powerful unquantifiable things in the world of business are fun and delight.

strategy.quote: What everyone really wants is clarity. And what everyone really wants is to be able to row in the same direction really quickly.
When people say 'I want someone strategic,' what they really mean is 'I want someone who can articulate a compelling why behind the decisions.'
Way too many founders apologize for how they want to run the company. That's a good way to make everyone miserable. Because what everyone really wants is clarity.

focus.quote: We took people away from the investment in our core product to go do those other things. The trap is that you leave yourself ripe for disruption.
It starts with adding little features here and there, making little cosmetic improvements until the next round of layoffs.
Your job is figuring out what the algorithm should be responsible for, what people are responsible for, and the framework for making those decisions.

metrics.quote: The algorithms don't understand long-term effects often, nor do they understand how people might respond to it.
There is no such thing as a long feedback loop. The way you choose to shorten it is to say, what are the things correlated with the outcome that I eventually desire?
Most people tend to ignore the non-determinism. You don't know how the user might behave with your product.

user_research.quote: Pessimism sounds extremely sophisticated. Optimism always sounds dumb or at least naive.
Every time you hand over decision-making capabilities to agentic systems, you're relinquishing some amount of control on your end.
I want someone that's going to champion and be a change agent to do things that may be hard but actually best for the long-term interest of the product.
